name: OpenAI vs Claude vs the rest
tagline: Everyone wants you to name the best AI. This lesson refuses — and that's the good news. There is no single winner: the big models are all excellent and they leapfrog each other every few months. The grown-up move isn't picking a favourite; it's matching the model to the job and staying free to switch. That freedom is the real skill.
overview: You came here for a straight answer to "OpenAI or Claude — which is best, which should I use?" Here's the honest one, said out loud: there is no single best model. OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini and the rest are all genuinely excellent, and they overtake each other constantly — so "best" keeps changing and always depends on the job in front of you. Chasing a permanent winner is a fool's errand; the useful question is "best for what, right now?" This module gives you a way to reason about it without any technical background, built on one everyday picture: asking "which is the best vehicle?" is meaningless until you know the journey — a van, a motorbike and a family car each win a different trip. Four things actually decide a model for a beginner: (1) the job — long careful writing and analysis, quick casual chat, coding, or making images; some models lean one way today, but all the big ones are strong; (2) cost and speed for how much you'll use it; (3) where your data is allowed to go — privacy and region rules; and (4) what you already have access to. You'll walk two everyday scenarios and see how the sensible pick can differ, without anyone pretending the winners are fixed. Then the three mistakes that trap beginners — brand loyalty, chasing every "new best" headline, and assuming the most expensive option is right for simple jobs — and why the professionals never marry a model. They stay portable. This is orientation and good judgement, not a leaderboard; nothing here asks you to be technical, and nothing here will go out of date, because it deliberately names no winner.
objectives: Explain, in one plain sentence, why there is no single "best" AI model — the major families (OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini, Meta's Llama and others) are all strong and they leapfrog each other constantly, so "best" changes and depends on the job.

Choose a model by reasoning about the job, plus cost and speed, plus where your data is allowed to go, plus what you already have access to — instead of by brand loyalty — using the "which vehicle for which journey" picture.

Recognise the three beginner mistakes — loyalty to one brand, chasing every "new best" headline, and assuming the priciest model is right for simple jobs — and treat model choice as reversible, keeping the freedom to switch as models improve and your needs change.

lessons.title: Why nobody can honestly name the best AI
lessons.objective: Understand plainly why there is no single best model — they're all strong and constantly overtake each other — and learn to ask "best for which job?" using the vehicles-for-journeys picture.
lessons.body:
## The question everyone asks, and the honest answer

Almost everyone new to AI wants the same thing from someone who knows a bit more: *"Just tell me which one is best. OpenAI or Claude? Which should I use?"* It's a completely fair thing to want. It would make life simpler.

I'm not going to give it to you — and here's why that's actually good news.

There is **no single best AI model.** OpenAI's GPT, Anthropic's Claude, Google's Gemini, Meta's Llama and the rest are all genuinely excellent. And — this is the part that matters — they **leapfrog each other every few months.** One pulls ahead, then another releases something new and retakes the lead, then the first answers back. It's a constant back-and-forth. So any sentence that starts "the best AI is…" is out of date almost as soon as it's said. Anyone who confidently crowns a permanent winner is either selling you something or hasn't noticed the race never stops.

That sounds like bad news — "great, so I *still* don't know which to pick." It isn't. It means you're freed from a question that has no permanent answer, and pointed at one that does: not *"which is best?"* but ***"which is best for this job, right now?"*** That one you can actually answer, and this module teaches you how.

## The picture to hold onto: which vehicle for which journey

Here's the everyday picture that makes the whole thing click. Imagine someone asks you: *"What's the best vehicle?"*

You can't answer it. Not because you don't know vehicles — because the question is missing the only thing that matters: **the journey.**

- Moving a sofa across town? A **van** wins. A motorbike is useless.
- Beating rush-hour traffic solo? A **motorbike** wins. The van is a nightmare.
- Family road trip with the kids and the luggage? A **comfortable car** wins. Now the motorbike's useless.

None of these is "the best vehicle." Each is the best *for a journey.* A van isn't better than a motorbike — it's better *at moving sofas* and worse *at filtering through traffic.* The question "which is best?" only becomes answerable the moment you add "...for **what**?"

AI models are exactly like this. GPT, Claude and Gemini are all superb vehicles. Asking which is best in the abstract is asking which vehicle is best with no journey named — a non-question. The skill isn't knowing which model is "the best." It's knowing **which journey you're on**, and matching the model to it.

## They really are all strong — and that's freeing

It's worth sitting with just how good the news is here. We are not choosing between a great model and some poor ones, where picking wrong means disaster. The major models are *all* excellent. For most everyday jobs, honestly, several of them would do a perfectly good job — the way a van, a car and even an estate could all move a few boxes if they had to.

So the stakes of "picking wrong" are low, and getting lower as they all improve. You are not defusing a bomb. You're choosing the *most convenient* good option for the task — and if you choose differently next week, nothing breaks. That relaxed, low-stakes framing is the right one, and it's the opposite of the anxious "but which is THE BEST" energy the marketing wants you to feel.

## So how do you actually choose? (the next lesson)

If "which is best?" is the wrong question and "best for this job?" is the right one, you need a way to size up the job. That's the whole of the next lesson: **four plain things** that decide it for a beginner — what the job actually is, how much it'll cost and how fast you need it, where your data is allowed to go, and what you already have access to. Four questions, no jargon, no benchmarks. Get those straight and you'll never be stuck on "which one?" again — because you'll be answering a question that actually has an answer.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why can't anyone honestly tell you which AI model is "the best"?

options.text: The major models are all strong and they leapfrog each other every few months, so "best" keeps changing and depends on the job
Because all AI models are actually the same product with different names on the box
Because they're all equally bad, so there's no point comparing them at all
Because there is a permanent best one, but companies keep it a secret from beginners

knowledgeCheck.explanation: There's no permanent winner because the big families are all excellent and constantly overtake one another — so the honest question is "best for which job, right now?" They're genuinely different products from different makers, not one thing relabelled (b). They're all strong, not all bad — the opposite of (c) — which is exactly why picking wrong is low-stakes. And nobody is hiding a permanent champion (d); there simply isn't one, because the race never stops.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What does the "which vehicle is best?" picture teach about choosing an AI model?

options.text: The question only becomes answerable once you name the journey — the job decides the pick, not an abstract "best"
You should always choose the biggest, most powerful option, the way a van carries the most
One vehicle really is best for every journey, so one model really is best for every job
Vehicles and AI models have nothing in common, so the comparison doesn't help

knowledgeCheck.explanation: "Best vehicle?" is unanswerable until you add "for what?" — a van wins for sofas, a motorbike for traffic — and models are the same: name the job first. Bigger isn't automatically better (b); a van is a poor choice for filtering through traffic, just as the heaviest model is overkill for a quick reply. No single vehicle wins every trip and no single model wins every job (c). And the analogy holds tightly (d): in both cases the job, not an abstract ranking, decides the right tool.

lessons.title: How to actually choose — and why the pros never marry a model
lessons.objective: Choose a model by reasoning about job, cost and speed, data and privacy, and access; avoid the three beginner mistakes; and treat model choice as reversible — keeping the freedom to switch.
lessons.body:
## Four plain questions that decide it

You never need to memorise which model is ahead this month. You just size up the job with four questions anyone can answer — no benchmarks, no jargon.

**1. What's the job?** This is the biggest one. Long, careful writing and analysis (a contract summary, a delicate email, reasoning through a problem) is a different journey from a quick casual chat, which is different again from writing computer code, or from making images. Some models lean one way today — but honestly, all the big ones are strong across the board, so this narrows the field more than it decides it. Name the journey first; everything else follows.

**2. How much will it cost, and how fast do you need it?** Using an AI isn't free, and the powerful models generally cost more and can be a little slower. If you're firing off hundreds of quick, simple requests, a cheaper, faster model is often the sensible pick — the motorbike, not the van. If you're doing one hard, important thing, paying more for a top model is easily worth it. Match the muscle to the moment.

**3. Where is your data allowed to go?** If you're handling anything sensitive — customer details, health or legal information, anything covered by privacy or region rules — then *where* the model runs and *who* can see the data can matter more than any capability. Sometimes the honest answer is "whichever option keeps this data where the rules require," and that decides it before job or cost even come up.

**4. What do you already have access to?** The best model in the world is no use to you today if you can't get to it. What you're already set up with — already paying for, already allowed to use at work — is a perfectly good default for most jobs. You don't need every option; you need a sensible one you can actually use.

Run those four and "which model?" stops being a mystery. You're not ranking the whole field — you're matching *this* job to a good-enough option, the way you'd pick a vehicle for a trip.

## Two everyday journeys, two sensible picks

**Journey one: a careful, semi-legal summary.** You've got a dense supplier contract and you need a clear, accurate plain-English summary you can rely on. This is the sofa-moving van trip: it rewards a strong, careful model, and the cost of getting one good answer is trivial next to the cost of getting it wrong. Reach for a top-tier model and don't fret about the price of a single job.

**Journey two: a hundred quick casual replies.** You're drafting short, friendly first-line responses to routine customer messages — high volume, low stakes, nothing sensitive, nothing subtle. This is the motorbike-through-traffic trip: a cheaper, faster model handles it beautifully, and using an expensive frontier model for it would be paying van prices to nip to the shops.

Notice what did **not** happen: I didn't tell you *which brand* wins either journey, because that moves. What's stable is the *reasoning* — heavy careful job leans one way, light high-volume job leans another. Learn the reasoning and you're future-proof; memorise this month's brand rankings and you're out of date by autumn.

## The three mistakes beginners make

Almost everyone new to this stumbles on one of these. Now you won't.

- **Brand loyalty.** Picking one company and using it for everything out of habit or team spirit — "I'm a Claude person," "we're an OpenAI shop." It feels like commitment; it's really just closing your options. The pros feel no loyalty to a logo. They use whatever fits the job.
- **Chasing every "new best" headline.** Every few weeks a breathless post declares a new king and insists you switch *right now.* If you jump every time, you'll spend your life migrating and never actually working. The leaderboard genuinely does change — but you don't have to chase it in real time. React when it matters for *your* jobs, not when the internet gets excited.
- **Assuming the priciest is the right one.** "It's the most expensive, so it must be best for me." For a hard, important job, maybe. For firing off simple messages, you're paying van prices to ride a motorbike's errand — slower and dearer for no gain. Expensive means *more capable*, not *more appropriate.* Match the model to the job, not to the price tag.

## The real skill: stay free to switch

Here's what separates people who are comfortable with AI from people who are anxious about it, and it's the whole point of this module.

The pros **don't marry a model.** They don't pledge allegiance to a brand and defend it. They treat model choice as **reversible** — a decision you can change tomorrow at no real cost, because you might, as the models improve and as your own needs shift. A cheap fast model for the routine stuff, a powerful one for the hard calls, and a shrug-and-swap the moment something better comes along for a job that matters to them.

That portability *is* the skill. Not knowing which model is best today — that knowledge expires in weeks. Knowing how to match a model to a job, and keeping yourself free to change your mind, never expires. So the honest, and genuinely useful, answer to "which should I use?" turns out to be: **match the model to the job, and keep the freedom to change. That freedom is the real skill.**

## The Automatos peek

This is the whole reason a platform like Automatos lets you use *any* model with your own key — because "which is best?" has no permanent answer, so the sensible design is to not lock you to one. In the workspace's model settings you can connect the big providers directly (OpenAI, Anthropic, and others) or reach a hundred-plus models through a single connecting service, and point different agents at different models — a cheap, fast one for routine work and a frontier one for the hard calls, side by side in the same workspace. You bring your own key, you can set a fallback so work carries on if one provider is busy, and you swap as the leaderboard changes. The honest answer to "which should I use?" — *keep the freedom to use any of them* — is built right into how the platform works.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You need to fire off hundreds of short, low-stakes, non-sensitive customer replies. Which reasoning fits best?

options.text: It's a high-volume, light job — a cheaper, faster model handles it well; a top-tier frontier model would be paying van prices for a motorbike errand
Always use the most expensive model available, because the priciest one is the best for every job
It doesn't matter which model — pick your favourite brand and use it for absolutely everything
Wait until the internet crowns a new "best" model this month, then switch to that one first

knowledgeCheck.explanation: High volume plus low stakes is the motorbike-through-traffic journey — a cheaper, faster model is the sensible match, and a frontier model would be slower and dearer for no gain. Reaching for the priciest option every time is exactly the third beginner mistake (b): expensive means more capable, not more appropriate. Defaulting to a favourite brand for everything is brand loyalty, the first mistake (c). And switching because the internet got excited is chasing headlines, the second mistake (d) — none of which is the same as matching the model to this job.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What do the professionals treat as the real skill in choosing AI models?

options.text: Matching the model to the job and staying free to switch — treating the choice as reversible as models improve and needs change
Committing hard to one favourite model and defending it against all the others
Memorising exactly which single model is best this month and using only that one
Always paying for the most powerful, most expensive model no matter how simple the task

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The durable skill is portability: match the model to the job and keep the freedom to change your mind, because the leaderboard keeps moving. Committing hard to one favourite is brand loyalty — a mistake, not a skill (b). Memorising this month's single "best" is knowledge that expires in weeks; the reasoning is what lasts (c). And always buying the most powerful option ignores cost, speed and how simple the job is (d) — the opposite of matching the model to the job.

questions.stem: Which statement most honestly answers a beginner asking "which AI model is best?"

options.text: There isn't a single best one — the major models are all strong and leapfrog each other, so the right pick depends on the job right now
Claude is best for everyone, so just use that and stop worrying about it
OpenAI's GPT is best for everyone, so just use that and never consider another
Whichever model is most expensive is the best, because price always tracks quality

questions.explanation: The honest answer is that there's no permanent winner — the big families are all excellent and keep overtaking each other, so "best" depends on the job. Crowning Claude for everyone (b) or GPT for everyone (c) is exactly the fixed-winner claim the module refuses, because the ranking moves. And price tracks capability, not appropriateness (d): the most expensive model is overkill for a simple job, so "priciest = best for me" is a beginner mistake, not the truth.

questions.stem: In the "which vehicle is best?" picture, why can't the question be answered as asked?

options.text: It's missing the journey — a van wins for moving a sofa, a motorbike for beating traffic; only the trip decides the winner
Because a van is simply better than every other vehicle at everything
Because all vehicles are identical, so any answer is as good as any other
Because the newest vehicle is always the best one, regardless of the trip

questions.explanation: "Best vehicle?" has no answer until you name the journey, because different trips have different winners — and AI models are the same, decided by the job. A van isn't best at everything (b): it's poor for filtering through traffic, just as the heaviest model is wrong for a quick reply. Vehicles clearly aren't identical (c) — each is suited to different trips. And newest-is-always-best (d) is the headline-chasing mistake, not how you match a tool to a task.

questions.stem: You're producing a careful, accurate plain-English summary of a dense legal contract. Which reasoning fits best?

options.text: It's a heavy, high-stakes job — lean toward a strong, careful model; the cost of one good answer is trivial next to the cost of getting it wrong
It's a heavy job, so pick the cheapest, fastest model to save money on this important task
It doesn't matter which model you use, because contracts are easy for every model equally
Use whichever brand you're loyal to, since loyalty matters more than the job at hand

questions.explanation: A careful, high-stakes summary is the van trip: it rewards a capable model, and paying more for one good answer is easily worth it when a mistake is costly. Choosing the cheapest, fastest option to save pennies on an important, error-sensitive job is a false economy (b) — that's the motorbike sent to move a sofa. It does matter which model here (c): careful analysis is exactly where capability earns its keep. And brand loyalty (d) is a named mistake — the job decides, not the logo.

questions.stem: You're handling customer records covered by privacy and region rules. Where does that fit among the four deciding questions?

options.text: "Where is the data allowed to go?" can outweigh job and cost — sometimes the pick is simply whatever keeps the data where the rules require
Privacy never affects model choice; you just pick the most capable model and ignore the rules
Only the price of the model matters here; data rules are irrelevant to which model you choose
You should always pick the newest headline model, since new models never have data concerns

questions.explanation: When data is sensitive, where the model runs and who can see it can decide the choice before job or cost come up — that's one of the four questions and it can dominate. Ignoring the rules to grab the most capable model (b) is exactly what privacy and region requirements forbid. Price is only one of the four factors and doesn't override data rules (c). And newest models are not automatically safe for sensitive data (d) — recency says nothing about where your data is allowed to go.

questions.stem: Which of these is one of the three model-choosing mistakes this module warns beginners against?

options.text: Brand loyalty — sticking to one company's model for everything out of habit instead of matching the model to the job
Matching the model to the job — which the module actually recommends, not warns against
Treating model choice as reversible — which the module presents as the right, freeing habit
Considering where your data is allowed to go — which is one of the sensible deciding questions

questions.explanation: Brand loyalty is one of the three named mistakes (alongside chasing "new best" headlines and assuming the priciest is right for simple jobs). The other options invert the module's real advice: matching the model to the job is the core recommendation, not a mistake (b); treating the choice as reversible is the freeing habit the pros use (c); and weighing where your data can go is one of the four sensible deciding questions (d).

questions.stem: A colleague says: "A new post says Model X just became the best — we have to switch everything to it today." What's the level-headed response?

options.text: The leaderboard genuinely moves, but react when it matters for our jobs — chasing every headline in real time means endless migrating and no real work
Yes — switch everything immediately every time a new "best" is announced, no matter the job
Ignore all new models forever, because the one we started with is the only one we should ever use
Whatever's most expensive after the switch must be the right call, so just buy the priciest

questions.explanation: The measured stance acknowledges the ranking really does change while refusing to chase it live — you react when it matters for your actual jobs. Switching everything on every headline is the second named mistake (b): you'd spend your life migrating and never working. Never considering new models (c) is the opposite error — brand loyalty and being left behind. And defaulting to the most expensive option (d) is the third mistake, confusing price with fit.

questions.stem: Why does this module deliberately avoid naming which model wins each example journey?

options.text: Because the winners move as models leapfrog each other — the durable lesson is the reasoning (heavy job vs light job), not this month's rankings
Because the author doesn't know anything about the models and is avoiding the question
Because the brand names are secret and can't be mentioned in a course
Because all models are so bad that naming one would be misleading either way

questions.explanation: Naming a fixed winner would rot, because the leaderboard keeps changing — so the module teaches the stable reasoning (match a heavy, careful job one way and a light, high-volume job another) instead. It's a deliberate durability choice, not avoidance or ignorance (b), and the brand names are used freely throughout, so they're hardly secret (c). And the models aren't bad — they're all strong (d); that's precisely why the honest advice is about matching, not ranking.

questions.stem: Which everyday factors does the module say should actually decide a beginner's model choice?

options.text: The job itself, the cost and speed, where the data is allowed to go, and what you already have access to
Only which company has the best marketing and the most exciting launch videos
Only the price — always pick the most expensive model and ignore everything else
Only which brand your friends and colleagues happen to prefer this year

questions.explanation: The four sensible questions are the job, cost and speed, data and privacy, and existing access — together they turn "which model?" into something answerable. Marketing and launch hype (b) are exactly what the headline-chasing mistake warns against. Price alone (c) is only one factor and, taken alone, leads to the "priciest = best" mistake. And your friends' preferences (d) are brand loyalty by another name — none of these single factors replaces reasoning about the actual job.

resources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide (choosing and using AI offerings)
resources.annotation: A vendor-light, business-toned primer aimed at newcomers. For this module, read the parts on choosing and using generative-AI offerings: they back the plain idea that model choice is a fit-for-purpose decision (the job, plus cost, speed and data considerations) rather than a hunt for one permanent "best" — which is the whole spine of this module. Beginner-toned and deliberately not tied to any one vendor's rankings.

resources.title: Automatos AI — Models (choosing and configuring providers)
resources.annotation: The peek's grounding: Automatos's Models settings let you connect the big providers directly (OpenAI, Anthropic and more) or reach a hundred-plus models through a single connecting service, set which model is the default, and configure a fallback chain so work continues if one provider is busy. Concrete proof that the platform is built to use any model and switch between them, exactly because there's no permanent "best."

resources.title: Automatos AI — Settings & Bring Your Own Key (BYOK)
resources.annotation: The workspace control panel where model and provider preferences live, including Bring Your Own Key — you can supply your own provider API keys instead of a shared default. Supports the module's closing point: keeping the freedom to use any model with your own key is a real platform setting, not a slogan. (Admin-only in a shared workspace; you're the admin of your own.)

videos.title: 04 · No single best — matching the model to the job, in 7 minutes
04 · Deep Dive — the four deciding factors and staying free to switch
